role: cross-check / second-opinion on the same two issues (ISS-1033, ISS-1034) addressed by EXP-166. EXP-166 uses the structured section_6_6_patch schema (new_tldr_html + new_prose_paragraphs_in_order) that the decider integrates from; this file packages the equivalent analytical conclusions as a single-block replacement_html. The decider should apply EXP-166 and treat this file as a corroborating draft, not a separate patch.
target: SEC-6.6 The Accuracy Claim — restructure to lead with internal inconsistency + preempt retroactive denominator defense
tldr_before: The accuracy claim is a tails-I-win, heads-you-lose system. The numerator is pumped by counting non-discriminating predictions — results that standard physics already explains — as "confirmed." The denominator is kept small by hiding failures: one acknowledged falsification is excluded from the falsified count, failed predictions are quietly "refined" or removed, and the accuracy arithmetic is never computed by any script — it's a static string someone types into the HTML. The headline number rewards the dome for agreeing with the globe model while penalizing it for nothing.
tldr_after: The headline accuracy figure disagrees with the dome's own internal data: the same repository produces four different accuracy figures depending on which endpoint and counting method you use, and none of them match the headline. That is possible because the figure is not computed by any script — it is a static HTML string, updated manually between versions. The denominator is kept small by reclassifying failed predictions as "refined" after the outcome is known — in clinical-trial language, a protocol deviation after unblinding.
replacement_html:
6.6 The Accuracy Claim

The headline accuracy figure disagrees with the dome's own internal data: the same repository produces four different accuracy figures depending on which endpoint and counting method you use, and none of them match the headline. That is possible because the figure is not computed by any script — it is a static HTML string, updated manually between versions. The denominator is kept small by reclassifying failed predictions as "refined" after the outcome is known — in clinical-trial language, a protocol deviation after unblinding.

The dome's own internal data does not agree with its headline accuracy figure. When we query the repository's own endpoints and apply their own counting rules, we get multiple different accuracy figures, none of which match the 94.5% headline: {{ACCURACY_VARIANT_DETAIL}}. The headline says 94.5%. Pick any data source the dome publishes and compute the accuracy from it; you will get a number, but you will not get 94.5%. That is the first thing to notice about the accuracy claim: it is inconsistent with the very data it purports to summarize.

The reason for this inconsistency is that the figure is not computed — it is written. The value sits in the homepage HTML as the static string <div class="score-number score-green">94.5%</div> and changes only when someone manually edits the markup. When V51.0 became V51.1, the headline fell from 95.2% to 94.5% in the same commit that added the two new WINs. A dome defender will correctly note that journal articles hardcode statistics in their abstracts all the time — but a journal's abstract figure is traceable to a specific method detailed in the paper. Here the dome publishes four plausible counting methods in its own data, and the headline matches none of them. The manually-entered HTML is not the problem by itself; it is the mechanism that makes the inconsistency possible. Without a script and an audit trail tying the headline to one of the dome's published counting methods, there is no way for a reader to reconstruct how the number was produced — or to know that the producer did reconstruct it.

We looked for such a script anyway. scoring.js (per-prediction point scoring, no aggregate), predictions.js (prediction data, no percentage), build.js (hash generation only), analytics.js (browser telemetry only), apply_scoring_schema.py, recalc_v51.py, compile_exhaustive_api.py, verify_predictions.py, build_tracking.py — none contain a function that calculates an overall accuracy percentage. The figure has no programmatic derivation and no audit trail in the codebase.

The denominator is an editorial choice, and the dome defender's best response is that every experiment has inclusion criteria — CERN excludes preliminary runs, medical trials exclude screen failures. The analogy fails on one specific point: standard practice requires the inclusion and exclusion criteria to be fixed before the outcome is known. The dome's excluded items (W019, W020, W024, W027) were not pre-registered as below-detection-threshold attempts. They were originally tracked as active predictions, with specific test windows, and were only reclassified as "refined" or moved out of the denominator after they failed — with labels softened from "falsified" to "refined" in every case except W024 (see Section 6.5 for details of each reclassification). In clinical-trial terminology this is called protocol deviation after unblinding, and it invalidates the analysis — not because the excluded items could never be legitimately excluded, but because the decision to exclude was made with full knowledge of which way it would move the percentage. If the same accounting were applied retroactively — counting all predictions the dome itself initially tracked as active — the figure would be {{TOTAL_WINS}} / {{HONEST_ACCURACY_DENOM}} ≈ {{HONEST_ACCURACY}}.

And even that corrected figure preserves the deeper issue. Accuracy metrics are only meaningful when predictions are independent and discriminating. As our point-by-point review shows, many WINs are re-sliced versions of the same data (WIN-040 through 043 are all SAA positioning from the same CHAOS-7 dataset), known physical constants (tidal periods), or globe values relabeled (Hubble Law, WGS84 gravity). A dome defender who argues that {{HONEST_ACCURACY}} is "still remarkable" for a cosmological model is counting predictions that any cosmological model — dome, globe, or otherwise — would also get right. An accuracy metric dominated by non-discriminating predictions is accurate-by-agreement, not accurate-by-prediction.
holes_addressed: hole 4 (moderate): restructure paragraph to lead with dome's own data contradicting its headline; make 'manually entered HTML' a consequence, not the lead
hole 5 (moderate): preempt retroactive denominator exclusion defense with protocol-deviation-after-unblinding framing
holes_not_addressed: hole 1 (critical): V51.0 arithmetic '{{TOTAL_WINS}} / ({{TOTAL_WINS}} + 4) = 95.2%' — my rewrite drops the V51.0 historical formula entirely, which removes the arithmetic-inconsistency symptom but does not address the underlying rendering bug if the template fragment reappears elsewhere. See follow-up issue suggestion.

hole 2 (major): '{{TOTAL_WINS}}/76 ≈ 88%' — my rewrite uses {{TOTAL_WINS}} / {{HONEST_ACCURACY_DENOM}} ≈ {{HONEST_ACCURACY}} which is computed correctly by the build script, avoiding the stale hardcoded 76/88. This incidentally resolves hole 2 in this section; any other section reusing the stale template string still needs a separate fix.
hole 3 (major): 6.6b De-Duplication table unclustered row count. Lives in a different <details> block (dedup-analysis), outside this section's scope.
hole 6 (moderate): add WIN-068 to eclipse magnetometer cluster. Data/build-script change, outside prose-scope of this EXP.
hole 7 (minor): tidal 'fair objection' tightening. Lives in a different paragraph, not targeted by ISS-1033/1034.
new_evidence_added: Protocol-deviation-after-unblinding analogy (clinical-trial methodology language) to defeat the CERN/medical-trial defense
Explicit list of the four retroactively-excluded items (W019, W020, W024, W027) with cross-reference to SEC-6.5 for per-item failure details
Explicit framing of manually-entered HTML as a CONSEQUENCE of the multi-figure inconsistency rather than as a standalone complaint
Acknowledgement that hardcoded stats in journal abstracts are fine when the counting method is traceable — closing the 'every journal hardcodes' defense

anticipated_objections.objection: Every scientific paper hardcodes statistics in its abstract. You are complaining about a formatting choice.
anticipated_objections.response: Journal abstracts hardcode figures whose counting method is traceable to the paper body. The dome publishes four plausible counting methods in its own data, and the headline matches none of them. Hardcoding is only a problem when provenance cannot be reconstructed — which is the case here.

anticipated_objections.objection: CERN excludes preliminary runs. Medical trials exclude screen failures. Every experiment has inclusion criteria; excluding low-confidence items is standard practice.
anticipated_objections.response: Standard practice requires inclusion criteria to be fixed before the outcome is known. The dome's excluded items were originally tracked as active predictions with test windows, and only reclassified as 'refined' after they failed. In clinical trials this is called protocol deviation after unblinding, and it invalidates the analysis — not because items cannot be excluded in principle, but because the exclusion was made with knowledge of the outcome.

anticipated_objections.objection: Even your 'honest' accuracy of ~{{HONEST_ACCURACY}} is remarkable for a cosmological model. You are arguing about 5 percentage points.
anticipated_objections.response: The corrected percentage is dominated by non-discriminating predictions — WINs that any cosmological model would also confirm because they predict known constants, globe values relabeled, or re-slices of the same dataset. A model that predicts what every other model also predicts is accurate-by-agreement, not accurate-by-prediction. The percentage measures bookkeeping, not predictive power.

anticipated_objections.objection: Your four different accuracy figures come from data endpoints with different purposes (scorecard, current results, open-problems inclusion, pre-V51.1). It is not fair to compare them as if they all answer the same question.
anticipated_objections.response: That is precisely the point. The dome does not disclose which question the 94.5% headline answers. Without the script and the audit trail, a reader cannot map the headline to any of the published methods. If the four endpoints answer different questions, the dome should show which one the headline reports — or compute it.

kernel_of_truth: The dome model maintains a prediction registry with timestamps and git hashes — which is better documentation than most fringe models provide. Having any falsifiable predictions at all puts it ahead of models that make only vague claims, and the percentage format, while inflated, is at least an attempt to quantify predictive success in a direct, auditable way. Good bookkeeping, however, does not validate the accuracy figure itself — it only proves WHEN predictions were registered, not THAT they discriminate between dome and globe models. And the headline percentage, in the end, is not tied to the registry at all: it is typed into the HTML by hand.
template_variables_used: {{HONEST_ACCURACY_DENOM}} — computed by build script: total + dome_claimed_failures + silent_failures
{{HONEST_ACCURACY}} — computed by build script: total / (total + dome_claimed_failures + silent_failures)
{{ACCURACY_VARIANT_DETAIL}} — computed from data/uncounted-failures.json dome_accuracy_variants.sources, joined with semicolons
arithmetic_safety_notes: This replacement removes the V51.0 hardcoded formula '{{TOTAL_WINS}} / ({{TOTAL_WINS}} + 4) = 95.2%' (curmudgeon hole 1 — the equation is arithmetically wrong when TOTAL_WINS=70) and the stale '{{TOTAL_WINS}}/76 ≈ 88%' string (curmudgeon hole 2 — 76 was hardcoded when TOTAL_WINS=67). The replacement uses only computed template variables, so the rendered page will always show arithmetically correct numbers regardless of how wins.json grows. Build script should be verified to produce a well-formed {{ACCURACY_VARIANT_DETAIL}} string at integration time.
cross_references: SEC-6.5 (<a href="#p6-65-4">) for per-item failure details on the four retroactively-excluded predictions (W019, W020, W024, W027)
Part 3 (point-by-point review) for the non-discriminating predictions argument
next_steps_for_decider: Replace the <details id="p6-66-5"> block in data/sections.json part6.html with the replacement_html above.

Verify at integration time that the build script still produces {{ACCURACY_VARIANT_DETAIL}}, {{HONEST_ACCURACY_DENOM}}, and {{HONEST_ACCURACY}} correctly (generate-html.js lines ~1086–1101 and failures-context section around lines 969, 1091).
On integration, close ISS-1033 and ISS-1034 as fixed (category: missing_argument → fixed, reference this EXP).

Consider whether curmudgeon holes 1–3 (arithmetic errors in other rendered strings and the 6.6b dedup table) need follow-up issues — my rewrite incidentally fixes holes 1 and 2 in this specific section but does not touch the 6.6b dedup table (hole 3) or audit for the same stale strings elsewhere.

follow_up_issues_suggested.title: Audit remaining hardcoded accuracy arithmetic across sections for template-variable drift
follow_up_issues_suggested.reason: Curmudgeon hole 1 and hole 2 identified stale hardcoded arithmetic in the V51.0 formula and in '{{TOTAL_WINS}}/76 ≈ 88%'. My EXP-165 removes these from SEC-6.6, but if any other section reuses the same strings they will still render with wrong arithmetic as TOTAL_WINS grows. Recommend a grep across sections.json for literal '= 95.2%', '/76', '≈ 88%', and any other hardcoded percentage/denominator pairs.

follow_up_issues_suggested.title: De-duplication table (SEC-6.6b) body-row counts drift from footer total
follow_up_issues_suggested.reason: Curmudgeon hole 3: the 6.6b dedup-analysis body hardcodes '29 WINs → 29 claims' for the unclustered row, while the footer uses the computed {{INDEPENDENT_CLAIMS}} and {{TOTAL_WINS}} template variables. As new WINs land, body and footer will disagree. Recommend making the unclustered row computed, or updating the dedupClusters array in generate-html.js so WIN-068 and WIN-069 are assigned to an existing cluster or declared genuinely standalone — whichever is correct on scientific grounds.

supersedes_prior_file: Previous monitor/analyst/expansions/EXP-165.json was an ID collision — that file's content (SEC-1.9 FSF citation pin, ISS-1009) was already integrated by the decider on 2026-04-14 and ISS-1009 is closed. The tracker reuses EXP-165 for this new SEC-6.6 target.
filename_rationale: EXP-165 was reused in the tracker for a new SEC-6.6 target after the original EXP-165 (SEC-1.9 FSF citation pin, ISS-1009) was already integrated on 2026-04-14. To preserve the append-only rule on monitor/analyst/expansions/, this file uses a disambiguating filename instead of overwriting the existing EXP-165.json. The tracker output_file field points to this file.
